title: Memory-assisted squeezed light velocimetry under realistic loss and incoherent noise
authors: Mustafa Gündoğan, Arash Ahmadi, Markus Krutzik
abstract: We propose a velocity sensor based on a two-memory Mach--Zehnder interferometer fed by a coherent probe and squeezed vacuum, read out by balanced homodyne detection. One memory is taken as a stationary reference, while the second memory moves during storage, so that its velocity is mapped onto a differential interferometric phase at readout. The two memories are otherwise assumed identical and are described by a Gaussian write--store--read lifetime together with the associated unconditional noise floor. Using the classical Fisher information, we derive the velocity sensitivity, the transmission threshold required for a target quantum gain, and the optimum storage time. The squeezed scheme improves on equal-resource coherent homodyne within an operating window set mainly by total transmission and phase stability. For representative near-term parameters, unconditional memory noise floors up to about $10^{-1}$ photons per trial do not by themselves remove the advantage; after optimization the improvement remains at the few-percent level and is limited chiefly by loss.
summary: This paper analyzes a velocity sensor that uses quantum memories and squeezed light to improve measurement precision. It demonstrates that while realistic losses and noise limit the quantum advantage to a few percent, the squeezed-light scheme remains robust and superior to classical methods under near-term experimental conditions.
main_problem: Investigating whether a memory-assisted velocimetry platform using squeezed light can achieve quantum enhancement despite realistic experimental imperfections like loss and incoherent noise.
main_result: The study finds that squeezed light provides a modest (few to ten percent) improvement in velocity sensitivity, and that this advantage is primarily limited by total transmission and phase stability rather than unconditional memory noise.
method: The authors use Classical Fisher Information (CFI) to derive the Cramér-Rao bound for velocity estimation and employ a Gaussian model for the variance budget.
model_or_system: A two-memory Mach-Zehnder interferometer where one memory is stationary and the other moves, mapping velocity to an interferometric phase. The system uses a coherent probe and squeezed vacuum with a Gaussian write-store-read lifetime model.
key_observables: Velocity sensitivity (Delta v), quadrature variance of the dark port, and the relative figure of merit (quantum gain).
important_parameters: Storage time (tau), memory lifetime (tau_mem), squeezing parameter (r), transmission efficiency (eta), and unconditional memory noise floor (pn).
main_assumptions: The memory-generated noise is assumed to be phase-insensitive and diagonal in the number basis; the system operates in a small-signal/linear-response regime.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the experimental schematic; Figure 2 plots optimized gain vs. memory noise; Figure 3 compares shot-normalized sensitivity for different detection schemes; Figure 4 maps gain as a function of transmission and squeezing.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the interferometric setup, defines a Gaussian noise and loss model, derives the sensitivity limits using Fisher Information, performs numerical optimization of storage time, and concludes with a comparison of different detection regimes and noise impacts.
why_interesting: It provides a rigorous metrological analysis of how non-classical states (squeezed light) perform in practical quantum memory-based sensing, specifically addressing the competition between signal accumulation and decoherence/loss.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Two-memory Mach-Zehnder model. A coherent probe enters mode ˆa0 and phase-squeezed vacuum enters mode ˆb0. Both arms are stored and retrieved in iden- tical memories with write-store-read eﬃciency ηmem(τ) = η0 exp[−(τ/τmem)2] and the same unconditional noise ﬂoor pn(τ). Motion of the upper memory during storage generates the signal phase ϕv = −kpvτ. Balanced homodyne detection measures the dark output.

FIG. 2. Optimized equal-resource gain versus unconditional memory-noise ﬂoor pn for 10 dB injected squeezing. Each curve is obtained after numerical optimization of Eq. (22) over storage time at ﬁxed shot number for a ﬁxed zero-storage transmission η(0). The vertical lines mark representative detector-referred noise ﬂoors reported or implied by Refs. [42– 44]. Over the experimentally relevant range pn ∼10−4–10−2, and even up to pn ∼10−1, the gain changes only slowly; in the reference working point the advantage is therefore limited more by transmission than by pn.
FIG. 3. Storage time dependence of the shot-normalized sen- sitivity ∆v √

FIG. 4. Best equal-resource gain after optimizing Eq. (22) over storage time at ﬁxed shot number, shown as a function of the zero-storage total transmission η(0) and the injected squeezing level. The reference point used elsewhere in the paper is marked. At ﬁxed transmission the gain saturates once loss and technical noise dominate the detected variance.

Figure 3 shows how the signal phase and the retrieval eﬃciency compete. At short storage times the accumu- lated phase is too small. At longer times the Gaussian decay of ηmem(τ) suppresses the retrieved ﬁeld strongly enough that the sensitivity rises again. In the constant- pn model this gives an optimum close to τmem for coherent homodyne and a slightly shorter optimum for squeezed input.
